region_of_origin: Indian subcontinent; widely consumed in Singapore

ontology.foodex2_description: Samosa — deep-fried pastry filled with spiced vegetables; mapped to FoodEx2 composite dish category for filled/fried pastries
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for vegetable samosa; A0CKP (filled fried pastry, composite dish) used as closest defensible parent analogue. Reviewer should confirm or substitute with jurisdiction-specific FoodEx2 code if available.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for vegetable samosa as of 2026.

main_ingredients: spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, ginger, green chilli)

serving.natural_language_portion: One medium vegetable samosa weighs approximately 70 g and is typically served as a snack or appetiser alongside mint chutney or tamarind sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Samosa — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists Vegetable Samosa (deep-fried, spiced potato and nut filling) with cr_id G00011066. All per-100g values are taken directly from this record. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry 'Samosa, vegetable' (FDC ID 2103705) as HPB does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions; this is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on deep-fried potato pastry (samosa-type) GI ~55
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No exact GI entry for 'Vegetable Samosa' exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: deep-fried potato-filled pastry (samosa-type), which appears in Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables with GI ~55. According to the University of Sydney GI Database, a GI of 55 is classified as 'low'.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 388 mg/100 g with low potassium (166 mg/100 g) and low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), compounded by deep-frying which adds saturated fat contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit vegetable samosa to one piece per occasion, avoid high-sodium accompaniments such as tamarind or mint chutney, and prefer a baked preparation to reduce both sodium load and saturated fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High available carbohydrate (33.2 g/100 g) from refined wheat flour pastry and high-GI potato filling, with low dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g) providing minimal glycaemic buffering; estimated GI ~55 (low) but portion effect elevates glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should restrict vegetable samosa to one small piece, avoid sweet chutneys that add further sugar load, and pair with a high-fibre side to blunt postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 17.47 g/100 g including saturated fat ~3.2 g/100 g and trans fat ~0.2 g/100 g from deep-frying, combined with sodium 388 mg/100 g and cholesterol 14 mg/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of deep-fried vegetable samosa due to its saturated and trans fat content; if consumed, limit to one piece and choose a baked alternative to reduce atherogenic fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; potato and green chilli are nightshade vegetables (solanine); trans fat 0.2 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried samosa and request a baked preparation; monitor for joint flare associated with nightshade ingredients (potato, green chilli).

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Samosa is a deep-fried triangular or conical pastry made from refined wheat flour (maida) dough, stuffed with a spiced filling of mashed potato, mixed nuts, and aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, ginger, and green chilli. It is a staple snack of Indian and South Asian origin, widely consumed across Singapore as a hawker-centre and restaurant appetiser or tea-time snack. The exterior is crisp and golden-brown from deep-frying, while the interior is soft, savoury, and mildly spiced. Per 100 g, it provides 310 kcal, 5.14 g protein, 17.47 g fat, and 33.16 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 388 mg and dietary fibre at only 1.8 g. The high carbohydrate load from refined pastry and potato filling, combined with low fibre, results in an estimated low glycaemic index (~55). The deep-frying process introduces saturated fat (~3.2 g/100 g) and trans fat (~0.2 g/100 g), raising cardiovascular and inflammatory concern. It is typically served with mint chutney or tamarind sauce, which can add further sodium and sugar. Clinically, it is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease without strict portion control. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-friendly, but contains gluten (wheat) and may contain tree nuts.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a samosa?
Is samosa good for high blood pressure?
What is the GI of samosa?
How much fat is in a samosa?
Can I eat samosa if I have heart disease?

confidence_reason: Core nutritional values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00011066, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore food records. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous entry and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry (deep-fried potato pastry) and documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (3.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for vegetable samosa (FDC ID 2103705) as HPB Foodo does not disaggregate fatty acid fractions for this record. 2. Trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) is carried forward from the input health_suitability annotation, consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for deep-fried wheat pastry; this value should be verified against a direct HPB or ICMR-NIN source when available. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this specific preparation not available in HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN as of 2026; USDA FoodData Central does not provide these fractions for the closest samosa entry. 4. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific HPB record are not reported; no approved source provides a sufficiently close match to populate these fields with confidence. 5. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on deep-fried potato-filled pastry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables; no exact samosa GI entry was found. According to the University of Sydney GI Database, a GI of 55 is classified as 'low'. Glycaemic load per serving = 55 × 23.2 / 100 = 12.7 (based on 70 g serving, 33.16 g carbohydrate/100 g). 6. FoodEx2 code A0CKP used as closest defensible parent analogue for filled fried pastry composite dish; foodex2_match_type set to 'closest_parent'; reviewer should confirm or substitute with a jurisdiction-specific code. 7. Cholesterol at 14 mg/100 g is consistent with the use of small amounts of ghee or egg wash in some preparations; the HPB record reports this value at confidence 0.95.
